title: Scroll, Contain, Type & Reveal
description: Five native web platform patterns — scroll-driven animations, container queries, variable fonts, cursor torch, and native dialog animations.

drops.title: Scroll-Driven Animations
drops.description: Progress bar, card reveals, parallax — all driven by scroll with zero JS. Pure CSS animation-timeline.
drops.concept: CSS scroll-driven animations landed in all modern browsers as Baseline 2024. Using animation-timeline: scroll() for a reading progress bar and animation-timeline: view() for per-element entry animations, this demo shows what took a full Intersection Observer + requestAnimationFrame setup is now two lines of CSS.

drops.title: CSS Container Queries
drops.description: Profile cards that adapt their internal layout based on container width — not the viewport.
drops.concept: Container queries (@container) let components own their responsive behavior. A profile card can show full detail at 600px wide, a compact version at 400px, and a minimal icon+name view at 250px — all based on the space given to it by its parent, not the viewport. This is the key shift from viewport-based to component-based responsive design.
drops.source: MDN Web Docs — CSS Container Queries

interaction.click: Move slider to see layout adapt

drops.title: Variable Font Playground
drops.description: Live weight, oblique, and tracking sliders on your system's built-in variable font, with a kinetic animation mode.
drops.concept: Your operating system ships a variable font: San Francisco on Apple devices, Segoe UI Variable on Windows 11, and many others. CSS font-weight can be any value from 100–900 and animates smoothly via the registered weight axis. The kinetic mode shows four words oscillating independently — the same technique used in editorial web typography today.
drops.source: web.dev — Variable Fonts on the Web

drops.title: Cursor Torch Effect
drops.description: A radial-gradient darkness overlay reveals hidden content as the cursor moves — four torch modes including auto-scan.
drops.concept: Two CSS custom properties (--x, --y) set by a single mousemove listener drive a radial-gradient overlay that goes from transparent at the cursor to near-opaque everywhere else. Three modes demonstrate the technique's range: a tight flashlight, a wide soft spotlight, and a rainbow conic-gradient glow. The auto-scan mode uses requestAnimationFrame for a roving torch effect.

drops.title: Native <dialog> Animations
drops.description: Modal, bottom sheet, and side drawer with smooth CSS enter/exit animations using @starting-style.
drops.concept: The HTML <dialog> element handles focus trapping, Escape key, backdrop clicks, and accessibility for free. But it couldn't animate because display: none can't transition. @starting-style (Chrome 117+, Safari 17.5+) defines the element's first-frame state so enter animations play, while transition-behavior: allow-discrete delays display: none until the exit transition finishes. Three variants show the full range of this pattern.
drops.source: Chrome Developers — Animate a dialog element

interaction.click: Click backdrop or ✕ to close; watch exit animation
